question: Moses and Aaron told Pharaoh: 'Let My people go that they may hold a ______ in the wilderness.'

memoryAid.text: Hear the demand: not merely 'let them go' but 'let them go that they may hold a feast.' Picture Israel leaving Egypt not just to be free but to gather and worship — the goal is the feast, the assembly around God.

question: Pharaoh responded, 'Who is the ______, that I should obey His voice?'

memoryAid.text: Pharaoh's question 'Who is the Lord?' is the defining posture of the prideful ruler who acknowledges no god above himself. In Egyptian theology Pharaoh was reckoned divine, so the plagues that follow are a direct contest in which the LORD executes judgment against the gods of Egypt (Ex 12:12), revealing Himself as the one true God. By the end, Egypt will know His name through judgment.
memoryAid.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, God Is a Man of War (Ancient Faith, 2021)

question: How did Pharaoh respond to the demand to let Israel go?
options: He demanded a tribute of gold
He agreed and then immediately repented

memoryAid.text: Picture Pharaoh sneering and piling MORE bricks onto already-bent backs. The first response to God's word is not relief but a crushing increase of the load.

question: Pharaoh ordered that the Israelites gather their own straw while keeping the same brick quota.

memoryAid.text: 'Same bricks, no straw, sore backs.' Same quota, but now they must find the straw themselves.

question: What building material were the Israelites suddenly required to gather for themselves while still meeting the same brick quota?

memoryAid.text: Straw for the bricks: 'No straw, same draw' — the quota is drawn up just as high as before.

question: Who were beaten when the brick quotas were not met?

memoryAid.text: See the Israelite foremen, caught in the middle, taking the lash for a quota no one could possibly meet without straw. The innocent middlemen bleed for Pharaoh's spite.

question: In Chapter 5, conditions for Israel grew worse before deliverance began.

memoryAid.text: Picture the night at its darkest moment right before dawn breaks. So in Egypt: the affliction grows worst right before God's mighty acts of deliverance begin. The increase of suffering is the threshold of redemption, not abandonment.

question: God reaffirms His covenant identity to Moses with the declaration, 'I am the ______.'

memoryAid.text: 'I am the LORD' (the divine Name) frames the whole chapter of promises. This is the same Name revealed at the burning bush, the 'I AM' that Christ takes upon Himself when He says, 'Before Abraham was, I AM.' The promises that follow are guaranteed not by circumstances but by the very Being of God who keeps covenant.

question: How many major promises did God make to Israel in Exodus 6?

memoryAid.text: Seven promises — the number of completeness and covenant. God's deliverance is whole and perfect, lacking nothing.

question: Which of the following is NOT one of the seven promises God made in Exodus 6?
answer: I will destroy all of Egypt's gods
options: I will destroy all of Egypt's gods
I will give it to you as an inheritance

memoryAid.text: The seven 'I wills': bring OUT, rescue, redeem, take, be your God, bring IN, give as inheritance. Destroying Egypt's gods, though it happens, is not one of the seven covenant promises listed.

question: In the seven promises, God says 'I will ______ you with an outstretched arm' — the act tied to the price of deliverance.

memoryAid.text: To 'redeem' means to buy back, to ransom. It stands among the seven 'I will' promises by which God binds Himself to deliver and claim Israel. God's redemption of Israel from Egypt prefigures the greater Redemption accomplished by Christ, who ransoms humanity from bondage to sin and death. The Exodus language of redemption runs straight to Pascha.
memoryAid.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, The Whole Counsel of God (Ancient Faith, 2021)

question: Among the seven promises, God said 'I will take you as My people, and I will be your God.'

memoryAid.text: This is the heart of the covenant formula and one of the seven 'I will' promises: 'I will take you as My people, and I will be your God.' It echoes through Scripture to the very last pages of Revelation, where God dwells with His people. The Exodus is ultimately about communion, God claiming a people for Himself.
memoryAid.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, The Whole Counsel of God (Ancient Faith, 2021)

question: Why did the Israelites not listen to Moses when he brought God's promises?
answer: Because of discouragement and cruel bondage
options: Because of discouragement and cruel bondage
Because they did not believe in God
Because Moses spoke in a foreign tongue

memoryAid.text: Picture a worker so exhausted by the whip that even good news bounces off him. Crushing bondage had hollowed out their hope; their spirits were too broken to hear.

question: The genealogy in Exodus 6 traces the lineage of which three sons of Israel?

memoryAid.text: First three sons in birth order: Reuben, Simeon, Levi. The list stops at Levi because the point is to establish the priestly line of Moses and Aaron.

question: Which of the following was NOT one of the three sons of Levi?

memoryAid.text: Levi's sons: Gershon, Kohath, Merari (G-K-M). Amram is a generation later — he is Kohath's son, not Levi's.

question: Which son of Levi was the father of Amram (and thus ancestor of Moses and Aaron)?

memoryAid.text: The priestly middle son: Kohath. 'Kohath the cornerstone' — through him comes Amram, then Moses and Aaron.

question: Who was the father of Aaron, Moses, and Miriam?

memoryAid.text: Amram + Jochebed = Aaron, Moses, Miriam. 'Amram's army of three.'

question: Who was the wife of Amram and mother of Aaron, Moses, and Miriam?

memoryAid.text: Jochebed — her name contains a form of the divine Name (Yah). The mother of the deliverer carries God's Name; she is the one who hid Moses in the ark of bulrushes.

question: Which of the following was NOT a child of Amram and Jochebed?

memoryAid.text: Children: Aaron, Moses, Miriam (A-M-M). Elisheba is Aaron's WIFE, not a sibling.

question: Who was the wife of Aaron?

memoryAid.text: Elisheba is the Hebrew form of 'Elizabeth.' The high priest Aaron's wife shares a name with the mother of John the Baptist — a wife linked to the priestly line.

question: Which of the following was NOT a son of Aaron?

memoryAid.text: Aaron's four sons: Nadab, Abihu, Eleazar, Ithamar. Amram is Aaron's FATHER, not his son.

question: How many sons did Aaron have, as listed in Exodus 6?

memoryAid.text: Four sons: Nadab, Abihu, Eleazar, Ithamar. 'NAEI' — say it like 'nay-eye.' Two would later perish offering strange fire; the priesthood continued through Eleazar and Ithamar.

question: Name the two sons of Aaron whose names begin with vowels and through whom the priesthood continued.

memoryAid.text: Picture Aaron's four sons in line: the two elder, Nadab and Abihu, will fall offering 'strange fire' before the Lord. The priesthood carries on through the two who remain, Eleazar and Ithamar — the vowel-named pair through whom the line continues.

question: God said, 'I will harden Pharaoh's heart and multiply My signs and My ______ in the land of Egypt.'

memoryAid.text: The hardening of Pharaoh's heart is a theme to treat carefully: God's persistent mercy is the very thing a hardened heart turns to its own condemnation. The same sun that softens wax hardens clay. Pharaoh's resistance becomes the stage on which God's 'signs and wonders' display His glory to all nations.
memoryAid.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, The Whole Counsel of God (Ancient Faith, 2021)

question: Aaron was 83 years old when he and Moses stood before Pharaoh.

memoryAid.text: Aaron is the elder: 'Aaron Ate 3 more' — A-8-3. Moses was 80, Aaron three years older at 83.

question: How old was Moses when he stood before Pharaoh?

memoryAid.text: Moses' life splits into three 40s: 40 in Egypt, 40 in Midian, 40 leading Israel. He begins the Exodus at 80 — two forties down, one to go.

question: When Aaron cast down his rod before Pharaoh, what did it become?

memoryAid.text: Picture Aaron's rod becoming a serpent and devouring the magicians' serpents — God's sign overpowering the very symbol of Egyptian royal power, the cobra worn on Pharaoh's crown. The serpent, emblem of the ancient deceiver, is swallowed up: the powers of Egypt are subject to God.

question: The Egyptian magicians were able to replicate the sign of the rod becoming a serpent.

memoryAid.text: Picture the court magicians smugly tossing down their own rods, each writhing into a snake — only to watch Aaron's serpent gulp them all down. They could copy the trick but not the power.

question: What happened after the magicians' rods also became serpents?
answer: Aaron's rod swallowed up their rods
options: Aaron's rod swallowed up their rods
All the serpents turned back into rods

memoryAid.text: 'Aaron's snake ate the fake snakes.' God's sign devours the counterfeit.

question: What was the first plague God brought upon Egypt?

memoryAid.text: The Nile was worshiped by Egypt as a life-giving god. By turning its waters to blood, the LORD strikes at the heart of Egyptian idolatry, executing judgment against the gods of Egypt (Ex 12:12). Each plague targets a power Egypt trusted, declaring there is no god but the LORD.
memoryAid.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, God Is a Man of War (Ancient Faith, 2021)

question: Which of the following was NOT among the waters struck in the first plague?

memoryAid.text: Struck: Nile, Streams, Canals, Ponds, Reservoirs. The sea is not in the list — it was all of Egypt's fresh waters that turned to blood.

question: Which of the following was NOT a result of the first plague?
answer: The land was covered in darkness
options: The land was covered in darkness
The Egyptians could not drink the water

memoryAid.text: Results of plague 1: fish died, river stank, no drinking water. Darkness comes much later, as the ninth plague.

question: How many days did the first plague (water turned to blood) last?

memoryAid.text: Seven full days the Nile ran red. 'Seven days of dread by the river of red.'

question: The Egyptian magicians were able to imitate the first plague.

memoryAid.text: Picture the magicians turning a little dish of water red to save face — adding to the misery rather than relieving it. They could mimic the plague but never undo it.

question: What was the second plague God sent upon Egypt?

memoryAid.text: The frog was associated with the Egyptian goddess Heqet, a deity of fertility and birth. By overwhelming the land with frogs, God again executes judgment against the gods of Egypt (Ex 12:12), turning a symbol of life into an unbearable plague. The judgment exposes the impotence of every idol.
memoryAid.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, God Is a Man of War (Ancient Faith, 2021)

question: Who stretched out his hand over the waters of Egypt to bring up the frogs?

memoryAid.text: Aaron holds the rod and stretches his hand for the early plagues; Moses speaks, Aaron acts. 'Aaron's arm brings the swarm.'

question: Which of the following places did the frogs NOT invade?

memoryAid.text: Frogs got into Houses, Bedrooms, Ovens, and Kneading bowls — even your bed and your bread. No relief anywhere a person lives or eats.

question: The frogs entered the Egyptians' ovens and kneading bowls.

memoryAid.text: Imagine reaching into your bread dough and pulling out a slimy frog. The plague invades the most intimate places: your bed, your oven, your supper.

question: The magicians were able to bring frogs upon Egypt.

memoryAid.text: The magicians could ADD frogs but never SUBTRACT them. They could imitate the plague but could not remove it — only God can deliver.

question: After Pharaoh asked for relief from the frogs, what happened?
answer: Moses prayed and the frogs died
options: Moses prayed and the frogs died
Aaron drove them into the sea
The frogs returned to the Nile alive

memoryAid.text: Only prayer ends the plague: Moses prays, frogs die. The magicians could start it, but only God through Moses could stop it.

question: After the frogs died, how were the dead frogs gathered?

memoryAid.text: Picture rotting frogs piled into stinking heaps across the land, the whole country reeking. Even the relief leaves a stench — a reminder of the cost of Pharaoh's stubbornness.

question: Pharaoh kept his promise to let Israel go after the frogs were removed.

memoryAid.text: The moment relief comes, Pharaoh hardens his heart again. This pattern of repentance under pressure followed by renewed rebellion when comfort returns is the very portrait of a heart that will not truly turn to God — a warning against false, fear-driven repentance.
memoryAid.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, The Whole Counsel of God (Ancient Faith, 2021)

question: What was the third plague, brought when Aaron struck the dust of the earth?

memoryAid.text: Third plague rises from the dust: lice/gnats. 'Dust to dust to bugs' — Aaron strikes the ground and the very dust becomes biting insects.

question: From striking what did Aaron bring forth the lice/gnats of the third plague?

memoryAid.text: From the dust man was formed, and from the dust the plague of gnats now comes. The lowliest element, the dust under their feet, becomes an instrument of God's judgment. This is the plague the magicians could not reproduce, confessing 'This is the finger of God,' for life from dust belongs to the Creator alone.
memoryAid.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, The Whole Counsel of God (Ancient Faith, 2021)

question: The magicians were able to duplicate the third plague of lice/gnats.

memoryAid.text: Plague three breaks the streak: the magicians fail. 'Three and done' — they could fake blood and frogs, but the gnats stopped them cold.

question: Unable to duplicate the third plague, the magicians said to Pharaoh, 'This is the ______ of God.'

memoryAid.text: 'This is the finger of God' is the magicians' confession of defeat: they could imitate the blood and the frogs, but not the gnats. The phrase points to the immediate working of God's power that no human counterfeit can match.
memoryAid.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, The Whole Counsel of God (Ancient Faith, 2021)

question: What was the fourth plague God sent upon Egypt?

memoryAid.text: Fourth plague: dog-flies / swarms of flies. 'Four for the flies that fill the skies.'

question: During the fourth plague, which region was spared from the dog-flies (swarms of flies)?

memoryAid.text: Goshen is God's safe zone. 'Goshen = Go safe.' The land where Israel dwelt was kept untouched.

question: The fourth plague marks the first time God makes a clear separation between Israel and Egypt, protecting His people while the Egyptians suffered.

memoryAid.text: Picture a clear line drawn across the land: dog-flies swarm everywhere in Egypt, but Goshen, where Israel dwells, stays untouched. Beginning with the flies, God 'sets apart' His people — a sign that the plagues are targeted acts of the God who knows and protects His own.

question: After the fourth plague, Pharaoh immediately allowed Israel to leave Egypt without conditions.

memoryAid.text: Picture Pharaoh bargaining: worship inside Egypt, or do not go far. Each offer is a half-measure, a leash kept on Israel — and then he hardens his heart yet again. True deliverance is a complete going-out, not a negotiated truce with Egypt.

question: Which of the following is the correct order of the second, third, and fourth plagues?

memoryAid.text: After blood (1): Frogs, Gnats, Flies — '1-2-3-4: Blood, Frogs, Gnats, Flies.' The magicians keep up through frogs, then fail at the gnats.
